medications.indication_rationale: The recently started beta-blocker potentiates peripheral vasoconstriction and is worsening the vasospasm. The correct action is to cease it pending GP review and arrange an alternative migraine prophylactic — continuing it perpetuates attacks. (De-prescribing decision to be confirmed with the prescribing GP.)
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise stopping the beta-blocker pending GP review; do not abruptly stop other cardiac beta-blockade without medical advice, but for migraine prophylaxis short-term cessation pending review is appropriate and the GP should substitute an alternative agent.

medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for the painful hyperaemic (rubor) recovery phase, as given in the source. Dose not specified in the source note and not synthesised here — standard OTC dosing to be confirmed at point of care.

Alternative simple analgesia for the throbbing hyperaemic pain, as offered in the source. Dose not specified in the source and not synthesised. Check no renal impairment, GI ulcer risk or NSAID intolerance before use.

A calcium channel blocker is the first-line pharmacological prophylaxis for FREQUENT/recurrent Raynaud's, but it is not the treatment for a single acute attack and should not be started acutely in this telehealth contact — it is a GP decision after in-person review and exclusion of secondary causes.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: At the very first sign of finger pallor, immediately place the hands under warm (not hot) running water or into the armpits to actively re-warm and abort the vasospasm before the cyanotic phase.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early active warming aborts the attack and bypasses the intensely painful hyperaemic recovery phase, reinforcing early intervention.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Purchase and use insulated gloves for all cold exposure, including opening the freezer, and keep the core body warm.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Thermal protection is the mainstay of preventing vasospastic attacks.

patient_education_points.point: The small blood vessels in the fingers are over-reacting to cold and clamping shut, cutting off blood flow (white), then the blood deoxygenates (blue); when they relax, blood rushes back causing the red, hot, throbbing pain.
The newly started migraine tablet (a beta-blocker) makes the clamping worse — it should be stopped pending review with your GP, who can switch you to a different preventer.
Run the hands under warm — not hot — water at the first sign of the fingers turning white to stop an attack early.
Primary Raynaud's is a lifelong but manageable condition; keeping the core and hands warm is the main treatment. Secondary underlying causes may need blood tests, so a GP follow-up is important.
Seek emergency care immediately if a finger stays permanently white or numb despite warming, or if any skin turns black or develops sores.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine General Practice review in 3-5 days (after the urgent same-day in-person perfusion assessment)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to warming and analgesia; review and substitution of the beta-blocker for an alternative migraine prophylactic; screen for secondary rheumatological causes (e.g. ANA testing); frequency/severity of attacks.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A digit that stays white/numb despite warming, any skin discolouration (black), sores or ulceration, or worsening/more frequent attacks.

must_include_items: Recognise this cannot be safely diagnosed remotely and arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment to palpate pulses and assess perfusion (exclude acute arterial occlusion)
Advise immediate active warming — hands under warm (not hot) water or into the armpits
Identify the recently started beta-blocker as an exacerbating factor and advise ceasing it pending GP review
Give explicit safety-net advice to attend the Emergency Department if a digit stays white/numb despite warming or the skin turns black/breaks down
Arrange GP follow-up for migraine-prophylaxis substitution and screening for secondary causes
acceptable_alternatives: Simple oral analgesia (paracetamol or ibuprofen) for the hyperaemic pain
Advising insulated gloves and core warmth for future prevention
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person assessment and reassuring the patient remotely that it is 'just Raynaud's'
Failing to ask about recently started medications and missing the beta-blocker precipitant
Omitting safety-net advice about persisting pallor or skin necrosis
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to 000 / ambulance for a reversible bilateral vasospasm that is already recovering
Starting a calcium channel blocker acutely in this telehealth contact
Advising the patient to abruptly stop a beta-blocker prescribed for a cardiac indication without medical advice
Recommending hot (rather than warm) water, risking a burn on numb digits
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the need for urgent in-person perfusion assessment (correct T3 disposition) AND identifying the beta-blocker precipitant. Confidently diagnosing benign Raynaud's remotely and discharging without in-person assessment is a critical under-triage failure; dispatching 000 is an over-triage failure.
